title: The Modes
subtitle: A small finite state machine you carry in your head
summary: Normal, Insert, Visual, Replace, Command-line, Operator-pending, Terminal. What each is for and how to move between them.

blocks.text: Vim has seven modes. You will spend essentially all your time in two of them — Normal and Insert. The other five matter for specific jobs. Knowing they exist, and how the transitions work, is more important than memorizing every command in each.

rows: Select text, then act on it.
Run an Ex command or search.
*(empty, but last key was an operator)*
Waiting for a motion or text object.

blocks.text: From any mode except Normal, {key:Esc} returns to Normal. From Insert and Visual, {key:Ctrl-[} does the same. Build the {key:Esc} reflex: after every thought, escape.
Terminal mode is the exception — {key:Esc} is forwarded to the shell. Use {key:Ctrl-\}{key:Ctrl-N} to escape from a Vim terminal.

If you want a visual cue for what mode you're in, configure your status line to show it. Most users learn the mode by feel — but for the first week, having `[INSERT]` or `[NORMAL]` in the corner removes a lot of confusion.

When you press an operator like {key:d}, {key:c}, {key:y}, or {key:>}, Vim enters *operator-pending mode* and waits for a motion or text object. You're in Normal mode no longer — keys do different things in this state. {key:i} no longer enters Insert; it begins a text object (inner ...). {key:a} no longer appends; it begins around-text-objects. The operator and motion together form a single change, which is what {key:.} repeats.

blocks.label: Operator d → operator-pending → text object iw → execute

blocks.title: The State Machine, Drawn
blocks.text:
The transition graph is small enough to memorize. From Normal you can reach Insert ({key:i}, {key:a}, {key:o}, …), Visual ({key:v}, {key:V}, {key:Ctrl-V}), Replace ({key:R}), Command-line ({key::}, {key:/}, {key:?}), Operator-pending (any operator), or Terminal (`:term`). From everywhere else, {key:Esc} returns you to Normal.

Visual has three sub-modes (char, line, block); pressing {key:v}/{key:V}/{key:Ctrl-V} while already in Visual switches between them. Insert has a sub-state called *Replace-Insert* (toggle with {key:Insert}) — you almost never want it.

The deepest transition is {key:Ctrl-O} from Insert mode: it pops you into Normal *for exactly one command*, then automatically returns. So {key:Ctrl-O}{key:dd} from Insert mode deletes the current line and continues typing where the new cursor lands.

blocks.title: Per-Mode State
blocks.text:
Each mode carries its own state. Normal mode tracks the count being typed (`5` then `j` is two keystrokes; the `5` is buffered). Insert mode buffers the inserted text so it can be repeated with {key:.} and so {key:Ctrl-O} can briefly leave and return. Visual mode tracks the *anchor* (where you started) and the *cursor* (where you are now); the selection is the range between them, recomputed every keystroke. Operator-pending tracks which operator is pending, plus its count and any selected register.

When the mode ends, this state is either committed (Insert → Normal commits the typed text and the count's repetitions; operator-pending → Normal applies the operator to the motion's range) or discarded (Visual → Normal without an operator just throws the selection away, though {key:gv} can recover it from the {key:'<} / {key:'>} marks).

When something surprises you in Vim — a key doesn't do what you expected — nine times out of ten you misjudged the current mode. "I pressed {key:dd} and it just typed two d's" → you were in Insert. "I pressed {key:i} and a word disappeared" → you were in operator-pending after {key:d}. The fix is always the same: press {key:Esc} (sometimes twice) and start over from Normal.
